title: Afghans biscuits
subtitle: New Zealand's chocolate cornflake biscuits with chocolate icing and a walnut
excerpt: A New Zealand classic: a crisp, lightly chocolatey biscuit made with crushed cornflakes for texture, finished with a soft chocolate icing and half a walnut on top. A fixture of New Zealand home baking.
sourceNotes: Afghan biscuits are a long-established part of New Zealand's baking tradition, appearing in the Edmonds cookery book (the definitive New Zealand home baking bible) since at least the 1950s, though earlier versions circulate in New Zealand community recipe collections from the 1930s. They are a specific New Zealand creation: the combination of cocoa, butter, cornflakes, and the chocolate icing with a walnut on top. The Edmonds recipe is now the canonical version.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The baked biscuits keep in a tin for up to 5 days before icing. Ice shortly before serving as the icing softens in humid conditions.

glossaryTerms.definition: Adding lightly crushed cornflakes to a biscuit dough creates a rough, irregular texture on the surface and a slightly crunchy bite in the finished biscuit. The cornflakes should be crushed only roughly — large fragments give better texture than fine crumbs.

content.text: Afghans are a forgiving biscuit: the dough comes together quickly by hand and the
method produces a rough, irregular surface that takes the chocolate icing well. Crush the cornflakes lightly, not to fine crumbs, for the characteristic bumpy texture.

attrs.prepNote: or enough to make a thick but spreadable icing

content.text: Preheat the oven to 180°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat the butter and sugar together until light. Sift in the flour and cocoa and stir to a soft dough. Fold in the crushed cornflakes. Roll into balls of about 30 g each and place on the trays, pressing down gently to flatten slightly. Bake for 13 to 15 minutes until set. The biscuits will be soft when they come out; they firm as they cool. Leave on the trays for 5 minutes, then transfer to a rack.

Sift the icing sugar and cocoa together. Stir in the melted butter and enough hot water to make a thick, spreadable icing. Spread a generous teaspoon of icing on top of each cooled biscuit and press a walnut half into the centre. Allow to set.
